nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical marine environments; typically found anchored in sandy substrates near reef flats, rubble zones, and sheltered lagoons

imageCandidates.notes: Ben Jobson; 2048x1535; in-water whole single plant anchored in sand, sharp and vibrant green, showing complete segmented/jointed calcified stalks each tipped with a green tuft — best full-specimen representation.
Ben Jobson; 2048x1536; crisp in-situ close-up of jointed calcified segments topped by bright green branchlet tufts, showing the diagnostic 'bearded joint' structure in vivid colour.
Ben Jobson; 2048x1536; sharp detail of the white calcified beaded segments with green tufts against a dark background, clearly illustrating the calcified jointed skeleton.

summary: Jointed Tuft (Cymopolia barbata) is a heavily calcified Caribbean green macroalgae with distinctive segmented joints, each tipped by a small green tuft of branchlets. It is generally considered an advanced species, requiring stable calcium, alkalinity, and high lighting to maintain its calcified structure.
careNotes:
Jointed Tuft (Cymopolia barbata) is a heavily calcified green macroalgae in the family Dasycladaceae, closely related to the Fern Finger (Batophora sp.) within the same family. Its most recognisable feature is a series of calcified, jointed segments each topped by a small green tuft of branchlets — the source of both its scientific epithet (barbata, meaning bearded) and its common name.

In the wild, Cymopolia barbata tends to occur in shallow Caribbean and Western Atlantic waters, anchored in sandy or rubble substrates near reef margins, lagoons, and sheltered coastal zones, generally from the surface down to roughly 20 m. It has been reported from Florida, the Bahamas, Cuba, Jamaica, Puerto Rico, Belize, Bermuda, and Venezuela.

As a heavily calcified green alga, Cymopolia is generally considered more demanding than most non-calcified macroalgae and more difficult to keep than its Dasycladaceae sibling, the Fern Finger. Stable calcium (400–450 ppm) and alkalinity (kH 8–12) are widely regarded as important for maintaining the calcified skeleton; fluctuations in these parameters tend to result in segment bleaching or tissue decline. High lighting is generally advised — low-light conditions are commonly associated with arrested growth or gradual deterioration. Growth rate is typically described as slow, consistent with the energy cost of calcification.

This species is sand-anchored in nature and tends to perform best when given at least 5 cm of sand substrate to anchor its holdfast.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole aquarium hobby source for this species. Product description confirms heavy calcification, segmented joints with green tufts, and sand-anchored growth habit.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Cymopolia
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmed: Family Dasycladaceae, Order Dasycladales. Minimal species-level detail available.

additional.name: AquaMate — Fern Finger (sw-algae-036, Batophora sp.)
additional.notes: Same family (Dasycladaceae) sibling entry used for cross-reference of water parameters, native range, and calcareous green husbandry conventions. Cymopolia barbata is more heavily calcified than Batophora, consistent with the advanced careLevel designation.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
